options.D: Prohibits disability discrimination in employment and requires reasonable accommodation

questions.explanation: The EU Employment Equality Directive prohibits disability discrimination in employment and occupation and requires reasonable accommodation.

questions.question: Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act requires accessibility for:

options.A: US federal electronic and information technology

questions.explanation: Section 508 requires US federal departments and agencies to ensure their electronic and information technology is accessible to people with disabilities.

questions.question: EN 301 549 is:

options.A: A European standard for ICT accessibility requirements
options.C: A United States federal accessibility law

questions.explanation: EN 301 549 is a European standard containing accessibility requirements for ICT products and services, aligned with WCAG.

questions.question: A harmonized European standard means:

options.A: Following it presumes conformance with related EU directives
options.B: It is mandatory for all businesses in Europe
options.C: It completely replaces all national laws
options.D: It only applies to music and audio content

questions.explanation: A harmonized standard is voluntary, but following it presumes conformance with related EU directives, simplifying compliance demonstration.

questions.question: The European Accessibility Act applies to:

options.A: Private businesses selling specified products and services

questions.explanation: The European Accessibility Act applies to private businesses (except microenterprises) selling specified products and services like computers, ATMs, e-commerce, etc.

questions.question: The European Accessibility Act applies from:
questions.explanation: The European Accessibility Act applies from 2025 to private businesses.

questions.question: The EU Web Accessibility Directive (2016/2102) applies to:

options.C: Public sector bodies' websites and mobile apps

questions.explanation: The Web Accessibility Directive establishes accessibility requirements for all EU member states' public sector bodies regarding websites and mobile apps.

questions.question: EN 301 549 extends WCAG's scope to include:
questions.explanation: EN 301 549 extends scope beyond web content to include downloadable documents, mobile apps, and additional requirements on biometrics and voice communication.

questions.question: The ADA Title III regarding web accessibility:

options.B: Does not include specific legal standards but organizations can still be sued
options.C: Has no application to digital content
options.D: Has specific legal standards for website accessibility

questions.explanation: ADA Title III does not include specific legal standards for website accessibility by private businesses, but organizations can be sued for lack of accessibility.

questions.question: The Twenty-First Century Communications and Video Accessibility Act (CVAA) updates accessibility laws for:

options.A: Modern digital, broadband, and mobile communications
options.D: Air travel and aviation industry only

questions.explanation: The CVAA updates federal communications law to increase accessibility in modern digital, broadband, and mobile communications technologies.

questions.question: The Air Carrier Access Act prohibits discrimination:

options.D: On basis of disability in air travel

questions.explanation: The Air Carrier Access Act prohibits discrimination on the basis of disability in air travel, applying to all US airline flights and flights to/from US by foreign airlines.

questions.question: The EU Audiovisual Media Services Directive requires:

options.A: Services made continuously and progressively more accessible
options.C: Primarily subtitles in the original language
options.D: Immediate full accessibility from the start

questions.explanation: The AVMSD requires audiovisual services to be made continuously and progressively more accessible through proportionate measures.

questions.question: The European Electronic Communications Code promotes:

options.A: High quality telecommunication services addressing needs of users with disabilities
options.B: Primarily mobile telephone services only without broader accessibility requirements
options.C: Primarily internet and broadband services without disability considerations
options.D: Primarily television broadcasting services without comprehensive accessibility

questions.explanation: The European Electronic Communications Code promotes high quality, secure, affordable telecommunication services addressing needs of end-users with disabilities.

questions.question: Procurement laws ensure accessibility by:

options.B: Applying to government purchases and related factors
options.C: Specifying accessibility standards that must be met when purchasing

questions.explanation: Procurement laws ensure user needs are accounted for when purchasing products and services by specifying accessibility standards that must be met.

questions.question: EU Public Procurement Directives require that technical specifications:

options.A: Ignore accessibility criteria entirely when creating technical specifications
options.B: Take into account accessibility criteria for people with disabilities
options.C: Apply only to physical products purchased without digital requirements
options.D: Focus primarily on cost considerations without accessibility requirements

questions.explanation: EU procurement directives require that technical specifications take into account accessibility criteria for people with disabilities or design for all.

questions.question: The US Federal Acquisition Regulation implements:
questions.explanation: The US Federal Acquisition Regulation implements Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act for federal ICT procurement.

questions.question: ICT accessibility should be approached as:

options.A: An ongoing program, not a project

questions.explanation: ICT accessibility must be approached strategically and programmatically - as an ongoing program, not a one-time project.

questions.question: According to W3C WAI, the four phases of accessibility implementation are:
questions.explanation: W3C WAI recommends four phases: Initiate, Plan, Implement, and Sustain.

questions.question: According to W3C WAI, the first phase of accessibility implementation is:
questions.explanation: W3C WAI recommends four phases: Initiate, Plan, Implement, and Sustain. Initiate comes first, focusing on building awareness and support.

questions.question: The Initiate phase includes:

options.A: Learning basics, developing business case, and raising awareness
options.B: Writing code, implementing solutions, and deploying accessibility features
options.C: Primarily monitoring ongoing compliance and maintaining accessibility standards
options.D: Primarily testing accessibility features and validating conformance levels

questions.explanation: The Initiate phase includes learning the basics, exploring the current environment, setting objectives, developing the business case, raising awareness, and gathering support.

questions.question: The Plan phase includes:

options.C: Creating accessibility policy and assigning responsibilities

questions.explanation: The Plan phase includes creating an accessibility policy, assigning responsibilities, determining budget, reviewing environment and websites, and establishing monitoring framework.

questions.question: The Implement phase includes:

options.A: Building skills, integrating goals into policies, and evaluating regularly
options.B: Policy creation, documentation activities, and establishing formal guidelines
options.C: Primarily planning, strategy development, and creating accessibility roadmaps
options.D: Primarily awareness raising, education, and initial stakeholder training

questions.explanation: The Implement phase includes building skills and expertise, integrating goals into policies, assigning tasks, evaluating early and regularly, and tracking progress.

questions.question: The Sustain phase includes:

options.A: Primarily initial development, launch activities, and establishing baselines
options.B: Monitoring, tracking standards and legislation, and adapting to new technologies
options.C: Primarily policy creation, documentation, and establishing formal guidelines
options.D: Primarily awareness raising, training, and initial educational initiatives

questions.explanation: The Sustain phase includes monitoring websites, engaging stakeholders, tracking standards and legislation, adapting to new technologies, and incorporating user feedback.

questions.question: In the Business Disability Forum's Accessibility Maturity Model, Level 5 represents:

options.D: Best practice - innovate, improve, and share

questions.explanation: Level 5 (Best practice) means the organization innovates, improves, and shares accessibility practices - the highest maturity level.

questions.question: Level 1 (Informal) in accessibility maturity models means:

options.A: No documentation or process in place

questions.explanation: Level 1 (Informal) means no documentation or process in place - the lowest maturity level.

questions.question: Level 4 (Managed) in accessibility maturity models means:

options.A: Process monitored and improved; business as usual

questions.explanation: Level 4 (Managed) means the process is monitored and improved, with accessibility as business as usual.

questions.question: Accessibility champions in an organization help to:

options.D: Build vision, sustain commitment, and lead adoption

questions.explanation: Accessibility champions build vision, sustain commitment, support integration throughout the organization, and lead maturity model adoption.

questions.question: Accessibility testing should occur:

options.A: Early and often throughout design and development
options.B: When legally required and related factors
options.C: Using automated tools and related factors

questions.explanation: W3C recommends evaluating early and often throughout design and development - it's easier and less costly to address issues early.

questions.question: Best practices for procuring accessible products include:

options.B: Verifying accessibility claims and requiring accessibility in contracts

questions.explanation: Best practices include verifying product accessibility claims, verifying vendor expertise, and requiring accessibility in contractual agreements.

questions.question: Human evaluation in accessibility testing is:

options.A: Needed for complex sites and related factors
options.C: Unnecessary if automated tools are used

questions.explanation: Human evaluation is critical for true accessibility assessment; automated tools cannot catch all issues.

questions.question: The 'born accessible' approach to development means:

options.B: Designing for accessibility from the start

questions.explanation: The 'born accessible' approach means designing and building for accessibility from the beginning rather than retrofitting.

questions.question: Creating reusable accessible design and code libraries helps to:
questions.explanation: Creating reusable accessible design and code libraries helps ensure consistent accessibility across products and reduces development time.

questions.question: Accessibility evaluations should include:
questions.explanation: Accessibility evaluations should include people with disabilities to identify real-world usability issues.

questions.question: To recruit people with disabilities, organizations should:

options.A: Avoid mentioning disability in job postings or recruitment communications
options.B: Include disability inclusion statement in job postings and ensure accessible recruitment
options.C: Primarily post on general job board sites without targeted disability outreach
options.D: Require mandatory disclosure of disability during the application process

questions.explanation: Organizations should include disability inclusion statements, post on disability-focused job boards, and ensure recruitment websites and facilities are accessible.

questions.question: Skills to look for in accessibility professionals include:

options.A: Knowledge of WCAG, screen reader proficiency, and testing skills
options.B: Primarily visual and graphic design skills without technical accessibility expertise
options.C: Marketing skills, brand management, and communications without accessibility focus
options.D: Primarily management and leadership skills without technical accessibility knowledge

questions.explanation: Accessibility professional skills include design, development, testing with evaluation tools and screen readers, and knowledge of WCAG and PDF/UA.

questions.question: Communication management for accessibility includes:

options.D: Publishing accessibility standards and ensuring document accessibility

questions.explanation: Communication management includes publishing accessibility standards, training communicators on people-first language, and ensuring accessibility of documents, websites, and media.

questions.question: Legal implications of accessibility include:

options.A: Identifying relevant laws, assessing liability, and documenting compliance
options.B: No legal risks for inaccessible products or services under current regulations
options.C: Primarily international law compliance without domestic legal requirements
options.D: Primarily government contracts requirements without broader legal obligations

questions.explanation: Legal implications include identifying relevant laws and standards, assessing legal liability, creating plans, and documenting how products meet standards.

questions.question: When procuring products, organizations should review:

options.D: Vendor accessibility roadmap for future improvements

questions.explanation: Best practices include reviewing vendor accessibility roadmap to understand future accessibility improvements.

questions.question: Types of accessibility evaluations include:
questions.explanation: Accessibility evaluations should include formative (during development), summative (at completion), and continuous evaluations.

questions.question: European Agency guidelines recommend developing:

options.A: Policies only when legally required to do so
options.B: Long-term strategy recognizing all aspects of disability

questions.explanation: European Agency guidelines recommend developing long-term strategy recognizing all aspects of disability.

questions.question: European Agency guidelines recommend providing:
questions.explanation: European Agency guidelines recommend providing awareness training for all staff.

questions.question: When working with external providers, organizations should:

options.A: Give them guidelines and requirements for accessibility
options.C: Primarily check final products for compliance

questions.explanation: Organizations should give external providers guidelines and requirements for accessibility.

questions.question: The UN Declaration on the Rights of Disabled Persons (1975) is:

options.A: Legally binding on all signatory nations with enforcement mechanisms in place
options.B: Primarily applicable to European countries and not other regions globally
options.C: No longer relevant to current legislation or contemporary disability rights
options.D: A non-binding declaration providing framework and recommendations

questions.explanation: The 1975 Declaration is a non-binding UN declaration providing framework and recommendations for disability rights.

questions.question: The CRPD is significant because it is:

options.A: Non-binding on signatory nations without legal enforcement requirements
options.B: Advisory in nature without legal force or binding obligations on states
options.C: The first binding international human rights instrument specifically for disability rights
options.D: Primarily applicable to developed countries without global relevance

questions.explanation: The CRPD is the first binding international human rights instrument specifically addressing the rights of people with disabilities.

questions.question: The European Union signed the CRPD as:

options.A: Not at all - EU did not sign the CRPD
options.C: A bloc, with member states ratifying individually
options.D: As an observer without full participation

questions.explanation: The European Union signed the CRPD as a bloc, with member states also ratifying individually.

questions.question: CRPD Article 9 requires states to provide:

options.A: Primarily technical solutions, automated systems, and digital accessibility tools
options.B: Physical infrastructure, building access, and architectural accessibility features
options.C: Live assistance such as guides, readers, and sign language interpreters
options.D: Primarily automated services, self-service options, and digital assistance systems

questions.explanation: CRPD Article 9 requires providing live assistance including guides, readers, and professional sign language interpreters.

questions.question: CRPD Article 9 promotes accessible ICT design at:

options.D: The final stage and related factors

questions.explanation: CRPD Article 9 promotes accessible ICT design at an early stage of development.

questions.question: The shift from welfare law to anti-discrimination law reflects moving from:
questions.explanation: The shift from welfare/social security law toward anti-discrimination/equality law is driven by the move from Medical Model to Social Model of disability.

questions.question: The goal of the ADA includes:

options.A: Medical treatment, rehabilitation services, and therapeutic interventions primarily
options.B: Primarily government services, federal programs, and public sector accessibility
options.C: Physical accessibility, building access, and architectural barrier removal primarily
options.D: Equality of opportunity, full participation, independent living, and economic self-sufficiency

questions.explanation: The ADA's goal is equality of opportunity, full participation, independent living, and economic self-sufficiency for people with disabilities.

questions.question: The EU Web Accessibility Directive requires:

options.B: An accessibility statement and method for reporting issues

questions.explanation: The Web Accessibility Directive requires an accessibility statement, method for users to report accessibility issues, and link to enforcement procedures.

questions.question: Products covered by the European Accessibility Act include:

options.A: Physical products and manufactured goods only
options.B: Computers, ATMs, smartphones, ticketing machines, and e-commerce

questions.explanation: The EAA covers computers, ATMs, ticketing machines, smartphones, TV equipment, telephony, e-books, e-commerce, and more.

questions.question: Under the European Accessibility Act, NGOs can:

options.D: Go to court on behalf of individuals

questions.explanation: Under the EAA, NGOs can go to court on behalf of individuals, and penalties exist for non-compliance.

questions.question: The eIDAS Regulation requires trust services to be:

options.D: For large organizations and related factors

questions.explanation: The eIDAS Regulation requires trust services (electronic signatures) and end-user products to be accessible for people with disabilities.

questions.question: Accessibility maturity models help organizations to:

options.D: Measure progress and improvements in accessibility

questions.explanation: Accessibility maturity models are tools for organizations to measure progress and improvements in accessibility.

questions.question: Level 3 (Repeatable/Defined) in maturity models means:

options.A: Process established, documented, and actioned consistently
options.B: No process has been established yet
options.D: Ad hoc only without consistent approach

questions.explanation: Level 3 means processes are established, documented, and actioned consistently across the organization.

questions.question: W3C WAI recommends engaging with stakeholders during:

options.A: Multiple phases including Plan and Sustain
options.D: When required by law and related factors

questions.explanation: W3C WAI recommends engaging with stakeholders during multiple phases, including Plan and Sustain phases.

questions.question: Accessibility champions help distinguish:
questions.explanation: Accessibility champions help distinguish accessibility programs from discrete projects, supporting ongoing programmatic integration.

questions.question: European Agency guidelines recommend conducting:
questions.explanation: European Agency guidelines recommend conducting a pilot of accessibility guidelines before full implementation.

questions.question: European Agency guidelines recommend producing:

options.C: Style guides and templates for accessibility

questions.explanation: European Agency guidelines recommend producing style guides and templates for accessibility.

questions.question: CMM Level 5 (Optimizing) for accessibility means:

options.B: Entire organization focused on continuous improvement

questions.explanation: CMM Level 5 (Optimizing) means the entire organization is focused on continuous improvement, identifying weaknesses proactively.

questions.question: CMM Level 2 (Repeatable) includes policies that are:

options.A: Primarily informal without documentation, formal procedures, or training programs
options.B: Not documented in formal procedures or established organizational guidelines
options.C: Not enforced consistently across the organization or between different teams
options.D: Practiced, documented, enforced, trained, measured, and able to improve

questions.explanation: CMM Level 2 includes policies that are practiced, documented, enforced, trained, measured, and able to improve.

questions.question: CMM Level 4 (Managed) includes:

options.B: Ad hoc approaches to quality management
options.C: No goals established for quality improvement
options.D: Quantitative quality goals for products and processes

questions.explanation: CMM Level 4 (Managed) includes quantitative quality goals for products and processes with well-defined and consistent measurements.

questions.question: According to WHO, at least how many people have vision impairment that could have been prevented?
questions.explanation: At least 1 billion people have vision impairment that could have been prevented or has yet to be addressed.

questions.question: Materials not available in alternate formats is a barrier in the:
questions.explanation: Materials not available in alternate formats (digital files, braille) is a physical environment barrier for people with visual disabilities.

questions.question: Canes as an assistive technology help people who are blind to:

options.A: Read printed or digital text content
options.C: Hear sounds and audio more clearly

questions.explanation: Canes help blind individuals feel their surroundings while walking, detecting obstacles and changes in terrain.

questions.question: Haptic alerts provide feedback through:

options.A: Sound and audio signals and alerts

questions.explanation: Haptic alerts provide feedback through touch or vibration, useful for people who are deaf or hard of hearing.

questions.question: Sound field systems help by:

options.C: Amplifying speakers' voices in a room

questions.explanation: Sound field systems amplify speakers' voices in a room, helping people who are hard of hearing.

questions.question: 70-80% of people with reading difficulties likely have:
questions.explanation: 70-80% of people with reading difficulties likely have dyslexia, making it the most common language-based learning disability.

questions.question: Dyslexia affects:

options.A: Similar numbers of boys and girls

questions.explanation: Dyslexia affects similar numbers of boys and girls.

questions.question: People with Autism Spectrum Disorders may experience:

options.C: Sensitivity to sound, smell, or light

questions.explanation: People with ASD may experience sensory issues including sensitivity to sound, smell, or light.

questions.question: Word prediction software helps people with:
questions.explanation: Word prediction and lookup software helps people with reading and writing difficulties, including those with dyslexia.

questions.question: Task management apps that break down complex tasks help people with:
questions.explanation: Task management apps that break down complex tasks into smaller steps help people with attention and focusing difficulties.

questions.question: A head wand is an assistive technology that allows users to:

options.A: Speak more clearly and articulate better
options.B: Control a computer using head movements
options.C: Hear sounds and audio more clearly

questions.explanation: A head wand is attached to the head and allows users to control keyboards or touchscreens through head movements.

questions.question: For people with mobility disabilities, interactive elements should have:

options.A: Primarily voice access, speech control features, and audio-based interaction options
options.B: Large enough clickable areas and not be placed too close together
options.C: Keyboard access, navigation features, and alternative input method support
options.D: Small touch targets to maximize content and minimize interface footprint

questions.explanation: Clickable areas should be big enough to hit, and interactive elements should not be placed too close together.

questions.question: Exoskeletons are assistive technologies that help people with:
questions.explanation: Exoskeletons are wearable devices that help people with mobility disabilities, including assisting with walking.

questions.question: When speaking with someone using a wheelchair for an extended conversation, you might consider:

options.A: Sitting to be at eye level

questions.explanation: If a conversation is lengthy and the person uses a wheelchair, consider sitting to be at eye level.

questions.question: If you don't understand what someone with a speech disability says, you should:

options.A: Ask someone else what they actually said
options.B: End the conversation to avoid confusion
options.C: Ask for clarification rather than pretending to understand
options.D: Pretend to understand what was said

questions.explanation: If you don't understand something, ask for clarification rather than pretending to understand.

questions.question: When giving directions to a blind person, you should use:

options.B: Specific language like 'the door to your left'

questions.explanation: Use specific language (e.g., 'the door to your left') rather than vague terms when giving directions to a blind person.

questions.question: When meeting someone with limited hand use, you should:

options.A: Avoid any physical contact with them during greetings or interactions
options.B: Assume they cannot shake hands at all and skip the greeting entirely
options.C: Offer to shake hands as people with limited hand use often can
options.D: Never offer to shake hands with them regardless of their capabilities

questions.explanation: Offer to shake hands when meeting someone, as people with limited hand use or an artificial limb can usually shake hands.

questions.question: A potential strength of the Charity Model is that it:

options.B: Can mobilize resources and support for people with disabilities

questions.explanation: While the Charity Model has weaknesses, it can mobilize charitable resources and support for people with disabilities.

questions.question: In practice, organizations generally use:
questions.explanation: In practice, organizations generally use aspects of multiple models, particularly the Social Model and Medical Model.

questions.question: Content that may trigger seizures should:

options.A: Have no warning provided to users

questions.explanation: Content that may trigger seizures should include warnings and provide alternatives when possible.

questions.question: The Seven Principles were developed by a group including:

options.A: Primarily medical professionals, doctors, and healthcare providers in disability field
options.B: Primarily software engineers, developers, and digital technology professionals
options.C: Primarily government officials, regulators, and policy makers in disability rights
options.D: Architects, product designers, engineers, and environmental design researchers

questions.explanation: The Seven Principles were developed by a working group of architects, product designers, engineers, and environmental design researchers.

questions.question: According to universal design, an environment should be designed to meet the needs of:

options.A: All people who wish to use it

questions.explanation: An environment should be designed to meet the needs of all people who wish to use it.

questions.question: Universal design is:

options.A: Primarily for compliance purposes only without broader design philosophy
options.B: Not a special requirement but a fundamental condition of good design
options.C: A special requirement for a minority of users without broader application
options.D: Optional for high quality products without being a fundamental condition

questions.explanation: Universal design is not a special requirement for a minority—it is a fundamental condition of good design.

questions.question: Equitable Use recommends providing means of use that are:

options.A: Special and separate for each user group
options.B: Identical whenever possible, equivalent when not
options.C: Primarily designed for majority users only

questions.explanation: Equitable Use guidelines recommend providing the same means of use for all users: identical whenever possible, equivalent when not.

questions.question: Flexibility in Use includes facilitating:
questions.explanation: Flexibility in Use guidelines include facilitating the user's accuracy and precision.

questions.question: Content that can be presented in different ways including by assistive technologies falls under:
questions.explanation: Creating content that can be presented in different ways, including by assistive technologies, without losing meaning is a Perceivable guideline.

questions.question: Making it easier for users to see and hear content falls under which WCAG principle?
questions.explanation: Making it easier for users to see and hear content is a Perceivable guideline.

questions.question: UDL representation includes providing options for perception through:
questions.explanation: UDL representation includes providing options for perception through different modalities.

questions.question: UDL representation addresses providing options for:
questions.explanation: UDL representation includes providing options for language and symbols.

questions.question: UDL representation includes building scaffolds for:
questions.explanation: UDL representation includes providing options for comprehension through design and presentation that builds scaffolds for knowledge.

questions.question: UDL engagement includes providing options for:
questions.explanation: UDL engagement includes providing options for recruiting learners' interest.

questions.question: UDL engagement includes options for learners to:
questions.explanation: UDL engagement includes providing options for learners to sustain their efforts.

questions.question: UDL action and expression includes options for learners to:

options.B: Write essays as the primary assessment
options.C: Express themselves and communicate in multiple ways

questions.explanation: UDL action and expression includes providing options for learners to express themselves and communicate in multiple ways.

questions.question: Universal design in the built environment includes:
questions.explanation: Applying universal design principles means providing intuitive wayfinding.

questions.question: Universal design in built environment includes providing:
questions.explanation: Applying universal design principles means providing clear sight lines.

questions.question: Built environment accessibility includes adequate space for:
questions.explanation: Universal design in the built environment includes adequate space for mobility devices.

questions.question: Universal design in built environment includes minimizing:
questions.explanation: Applying universal design principles means minimizing physical effort required.

questions.question: One key aspect of usability is that users should:

options.B: Find it easy to become proficient using a design interface
options.D: Struggle initially when first using it

questions.explanation: One key aspect of usability is that users should find it easy to become proficient using a design interface.

questions.question: Core UX includes providing useful content that offers:

options.A: Minimal information to reduce overload without enabling informed decisions
options.B: Enough information in digestible format for informed decisions
options.C: Primarily promotional marketing content without substantive user information
options.D: Primarily technical details and specifications without user-friendly format

questions.explanation: Useful content in UX means providing enough information in a digestible format for users to make informed decisions.

questions.question: UX comes from:

options.A: Development processes, methodologies, and technical implementation alone
options.B: Graphical design, interactive design, content, and usability combined
options.C: Usability considerations alone primarily without other contributing factors
options.D: Marketing activities, related efforts, and promotional communications alone

questions.explanation: UX comes from graphical design, interactive design, content, and usability combined, not from any single element alone.

questions.question: User-centered design includes:

options.A: Conducting user research and involving users throughout

questions.explanation: User-centered design includes conducting user research and involving users from beginning throughout design and development.

questions.question: Accessibility measurement typically includes:
questions.explanation: Accessibility measurement typically includes conformance to standards like WCAG.

questions.question: Usability measurement includes:

options.A: Task completion, error rates, and satisfaction

questions.explanation: Usability measurement includes task completion, error rates, and user satisfaction.

questions.question: User experience measurement includes:
questions.explanation: UX measurement includes overall satisfaction, trust, and engagement.

questions.question: Best practice for accessibility includes building it into:
questions.explanation: Best practice includes building accessibility into design systems and patterns.

questions.question: Web accessibility benefits people using:

options.C: Mobile phones, smart watches, smart TVs with small screens

questions.explanation: Web accessibility benefits people using mobile phones, smart watches, smart TVs, and other devices with small screens.

questions.question: Web accessibility benefits people with temporary disabilities such as:

options.A: A broken arm or lost glasses

questions.explanation: Web accessibility benefits people with temporary disabilities such as a broken arm or lost glasses.

questions.question: Web accessibility benefits people with situational limitations such as:
questions.explanation: Web accessibility benefits people with situational limitations such as bright sunlight or noisy environments.

questions.question: Web accessibility also benefits people with:

options.A: Slow internet connections or limited bandwidth
options.B: Primarily users with new devices only
options.C: Primarily users with fast internet only

questions.explanation: Web accessibility benefits people using slow internet connections or those with limited bandwidth.

questions.question: Accessibility enables people with disabilities to have:
questions.explanation: Accessibility enables people with disabilities to have independence in daily activities.

questions.question: Cluttered design creates barriers for people with cognitive disabilities by causing:
questions.explanation: Cluttered design causes information overload, creating barriers for people with cognitive disabilities.

questions.question: Mindfulness and anxiety management apps are assistive technologies for:
questions.explanation: Mindfulness and anxiety management apps are assistive technologies supporting emotional functions.

questions.question: Direction-finding applications are assistive technologies for:
questions.explanation: Direction-finding applications are assistive technologies supporting perceptual functions.

questions.question: Speech-recognition software helps people with cognitive disabilities by:

options.D: Allowing them to write using voice instead of typing

questions.explanation: Speech-recognition software allows people to write using voice, helping those with reading and writing difficulties.

questions.question: Body size or shape disabilities include conditions such as:
questions.explanation: Body size or shape disabilities include conditions such as dwarfism, acromegaly, rheumatoid arthritis, and obesity.

questions.question: Ataxia is associated with:
questions.explanation: Ataxia results from neurological damage (from stroke, cerebral palsy, Multiple Sclerosis) and affects motor control and dexterity.

questions.question: An oversized trackball is an assistive technology for people with:

options.C: Mobility disabilities affecting fine motor control

questions.explanation: An oversized mouse or trackball is an assistive technology for people with mobility disabilities affecting fine motor control.

questions.question: Tables without knee and toe clearance are barriers for:
questions.explanation: Tables without knee and toe clearance are barriers for people who use wheelchairs.

questions.question: Central Auditory Processing Disorder is often confused with:

options.A: ADHD, language impairment, or learning disabilities

questions.explanation: APD is often confused with ADHD, language impairment, or learning disabilities because of similar symptoms.

questions.question: Environments with good acoustics help people who are:
questions.explanation: Environments with good acoustics and lighting help people who are hard of hearing.

questions.question: Flexibility in Use includes providing:
questions.explanation: Flexibility in Use guidelines include providing choice in methods of use.

questions.question: WCAG Level AA is:

options.B: The mid-range level commonly required by laws and policies

questions.explanation: WCAG Level AA is the mid-range conformance level commonly required by accessibility laws and policies.

questions.question: WCAG Level AAA represents:
questions.explanation: WCAG Level AAA represents the highest level of conformance with the most stringent success criteria.

questions.question: UDL guidelines are based on principles of:
questions.explanation: UDL is a set of guidelines based on principles of cognitive science emphasizing flexibility.

questions.question: UDL engagement recognizes that some learners are engaged by:
